skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Automate browser sessions through inference.sh with Playwright-style navigation, element refs, form interaction, screenshots, video recording, proxy routing, file upload, and page JavaScript execution.
skill.summary: Control browser sessions with refs, screenshots, video, proxies, uploads, and page JavaScript.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Most shell-command findings are documentation examples or expected infsh template calls, but the skill intentionally supports authenticated browsing, arbitrary page JavaScript, screenshots, video capture, file upload, and proxy routing. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but the capability set is high risk and needs human marketplace review before publication.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
high_findings.description: The skill exposes an execute function that runs caller-supplied JavaScript in the active browser page. This can extract page text, links, cookies exposed to JavaScript, and sensitive authenticated page data.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The function is explicitly documented as running custom JavaScript and the templates use it for page extraction. The risk is confirmed, although it is an intended browser automation capability rather than hidden malicious code.

high_findings.title: Cookie and Authenticated Session Extraction Guidance
high_findings.description: The authentication reference documents login automation, session reuse, and cookie extraction. This can expose session cookies or authenticated content if used on sensitive sites.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly includes a Cookie Extraction section and examples that read document.cookie. The code is instructional, but the security impact is clear for marketplace users.

high_findings.title: Proxy Routing and Rate Limit Avoidance Workflows
high_findings.description: The proxy documentation supports authenticated proxies, SOCKS5 routing, privacy proxies, and rotating proxies for scraping. These features can support legitimate testing, but they also enable evasive browsing and abuse at scale.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The proxy and rotation workflows are explicitly documented. The risk is high because the guidance includes rate limit avoidance, while legitimate geo-testing remains possible.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Screenshot and Video Capture
high_findings.description: The skill can capture screenshots and full-session video, including authenticated pages, typed input, and admin workflows. The documentation warns against recording sensitive sessions, but capture remains a core capability.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Screenshots and videos are documented core outputs, and examples include admin and audit workflows. The risk is confirmed because visual captures can contain credentials or private data.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability
medium_findings.description: The interact function supports uploading caller-specified file paths to browser file inputs. This is legitimate for testing, but an agent could upload sensitive local files if given unsafe paths or instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload action and file_paths parameter are explicitly documented. The risk depends on caller control and user consent, so medium severity is appropriate.

medium_findings.title: Template Shell Calls With User-Supplied URLs and Output Paths
medium_findings.description: The shell templates pass command-line URL and output-directory inputs into infsh workflows and write capture files. This is expected for the templates, but users must avoid untrusted targets and output paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The templates clearly consume user-provided URLs and paths. I did not find direct shell command injection, but the behavior increases operational risk when inputs are untrusted.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Mostly Expected Examples
low_findings.description: The many external command detections occur in Markdown examples and shell templates that call the declared infsh browser CLI. They are not evidence of hidden command execution or obfuscation by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanned content shows examples and templates centered on the declared allowed tool. The volume of hits is real, but the intent is documented browser automation.

low_findings.title: Markdown Relative Links Triggered Path Traversal Heuristics
low_findings.description: Path traversal detections are caused by documentation links such as ../SKILL.md. I did not find evidence that these links are used to read arbitrary files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation links, not filesystem access logic. This supports dismissing the path traversal findings as false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard infsh Bash Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requests Bash access for all infsh commands. That scope is broad enough to invoke any inference.sh CLI operation available to the user.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit. The risk depends on the infsh account permissions and available apps, so confidence is high but not absolute.

dangerous_patterns.title: Execute Function Accepts JavaScript Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The command reference defines a code field for JavaScript execution on the page and returns the result plus a screenshot.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The input field and output behavior are directly documented. This is a confirmed powerful pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cookie Extraction Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The authentication reference includes an example that executes document.cookie and prints the resulting cookies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section title and example directly describe cookie extraction. This is the clearest sensitive-data pattern in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: SOCKS and Rotating Proxy Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The proxy reference documents SOCKS5 proxies and rotating proxy lists for scraping and rate limit avoidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit and include rate limit avoidance language. Legitimate testing is possible, but the abuse potential is concrete.

dangerous_patterns.title: Screenshot and Video Artifact Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill returns screenshots and video files from browser sessions, including full-page screenshots and full-session recordings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Capture behavior is repeatedly documented and includes best-practice warnings. The pattern is confirmed and sensitive-data exposure is plausible.

content.value_statement: Manual web interaction slows research, testing, and data collection. This skill lets Claude, Codex, and Claude Code drive browser sessions through inference.sh with refs, screenshots, video, and extraction tools.
actual_capabilities: Open web pages and return interactive element refs for clicks, fills, selections, and navigation.
Refresh snapshots after page changes so agents can use current element refs.
Capture viewport or full-page screenshots and optional session videos.
Run JavaScript in the page to extract text, links, metadata, or computed values.
Automate forms, authenticated sessions, drag-and-drop actions, scrolling, and file uploads.
Route browser traffic through HTTP, HTTPS, or SOCKS5 proxies when configured.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and a working infsh login.
Element refs can become stale after navigation or dynamic page updates.
Sensitive pages can expose credentials, cookies, screenshots, or video artifacts.
Proxy, scraping, and authenticated automation must follow site terms and user permissions.

use_cases.title: Validate web forms
use_cases.description: Open a form, inspect refs, submit test data, and verify the resulting page state.

use_cases.title: Collect structured page evidence
use_cases.description: Capture text, links, screenshots, and videos from public web pages for review.

use_cases.title: Debug authenticated workflows
use_cases.description: Use a controlled login session to reproduce navigation issues and save visual evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Open and summarize a page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this URL with agent-browser, list the visible interactive elements, and summarize the page purpose: <url>

prompt_templates.title: Fill a test form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to inspect this form, identify the required refs, fill safe test data, submit it, and report the final page state: <url>

prompt_templates.title: Capture research evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Open <url>, capture a full-page screenshot, extract the main text and outbound links, then close the session.

prompt_templates.title: Record a debug workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a recorded browser session for <url>, perform the specified workflow using current refs, redact sensitive values in the report, and close the session to return the video artifact.

output_examples.input: Inspect a landing page and identify key controls.
output_examples.output: The result lists the page title, current URL, visible buttons, input fields, links, and a screenshot reference for review.

output_examples.input: Submit a contact form with test values.
output_examples.output: The result explains which refs were used, whether the submission appeared successful, and what confirmation text or error state was visible.

output_examples.input: Capture evidence from a public documentation page.
output: Main text and links are extracted.
The browser session is closed cleanly.

best_practices: Use fresh snapshots after navigation, form submission, scrolling, or dynamic updates.
Avoid recording or screenshotting pages that contain credentials, tokens, private records, or customer data.
Use test accounts, explicit user permission, and site-compliant rate limits for automation.
anti_patterns: Do not use stale refs after a page changes or reloads.
Do not extract cookies, private data, or authenticated content without explicit authorization.
Do not use proxy rotation to bypass access controls, bans, or rate limits.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, element interaction, screenshots, video recording, file upload, proxy use, and page JavaScript through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it require setup?
faq.answer: Yes. Users need the inference.sh CLI, an infsh login, and access to the agent-browser app.

faq.question: Can it work with authenticated sites?
faq.answer: Yes, but use test accounts and avoid recording or extracting sensitive session data.

faq.question: Why do refs need refreshing?
faq.answer: Refs map to the current page state. Navigation or dynamic updates can make old refs point to the wrong element.

faq.question: Can it scrape websites?
faq.answer: It can extract page text and links, but users must respect site terms, privacy rules, and rate limits.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: It can execute page JavaScript, capture sensitive visuals, handle credentials, upload files, and route traffic through proxies.
